title: ISRO Robotics
oneLiner: India's space agency and its robotic missions — Chandrayaan, Gaganyaan, Vyommitra.
layman: ISRO builds robots that operate in space — rovers that explore the Moon, robotic arms on satellites, and humanoid robots being tested for the Gaganyaan crewed mission.
analogy: Think of ISRO as a national-scale robotics workshop where every product has to work the first time, in vacuum, with 20-minute communication delays.
why: ISRO is India's flagship robotics employer and the most credible Indian organisation building world-class robotic systems. Every successful mission strengthens India's strategic, scientific, and commercial space posture.
indianEx: Chandrayaan-3's Pragyan rover (August 2023) — India's first mobile robot on the Moon — covered roughly 100 metres in 14 days while sending back surface data. The mission cost ~₹600 crore total, less than a single Hollywood film.
quizQ: What is Vyommitra and why is it important?
quizA: Vyommitra is ISRO's humanoid robot designed to test the Gaganyaan crew capsule's life-support and control systems before sending human astronauts — half-humanoid (no legs), with face, arms, and ability to operate cabin controls.
mindBlow: Chandrayaan-3 made India the first country to soft-land near the Moon's south pole — a region of strategic interest because of suspected water ice. The entire mission cost about $75 million, less than the budget of many recent space movies.
body:
ISRO's robotics work spans planetary rovers, satellite mechanisms (deployment systems, robotic arms on next-gen platforms), humanoid validation systems (Vyommitra), and increasingly Earth-orbit servicing. The work is distributed across URSC Bengaluru (satellite mechanisms and rovers), VSSC Thiruvananthapuram (propulsion and avionics), and SAC Ahmedabad (payloads).

Chandrayaan-3's success in 2023 was a robotic landmark — soft-landing requires hundreds of simultaneous decisions in the final 17 minutes, all autonomous. Pragyan's surface mobility was modest (a few metres at a time) but successful in a way many previous lunar rovers were not.

The pipeline ahead is dense. Chandrayaan-4 (lunar sample return), Gaganyaan (crewed flight with Vyommitra), the proposed Bharatiya Antariksh Station in 2035, and an active commercial space sector (Skyroot, Agnikul, Pixxel, Bellatrix Aerospace) all need increasing robotic capability. ISRO is increasingly partnering with Indian industry rather than building everything in-house, which broadens the addressable robotics ecosystem dramatically.

title: DRDO Robotics
oneLiner: India's defence research and its robot systems — Daksh, mine detectors, autonomous platforms.
layman: DRDO is the research arm of India's defence ministry. Its robotics work includes bomb-disposal robots (Daksh), mine-detection rovers, surveillance drones, and increasingly autonomous ground and aerial platforms for military use.
analogy: Think of DRDO as the engineering team that builds the dangerous-job robots — the ones that go where Indian soldiers shouldn't have to.
why: Indigenous defence robotics is a strategic capability — relying on imports for bomb-disposal robots or surveillance drones creates supply-chain risk in conflict. DRDO's robotics work also feeds commercial spin-offs into Indian industry.
indianEx: DRDO's Daksh — developed at R&DE(E) Pune — is a remote-controlled tracked robot for IED disposal. Over 60 units serve with the Indian Army's Corps of Engineers, primarily in Kashmir and the North-East. Daksh has saved an unknown but substantial number of EOD operator lives.
quizQ: What's the typical DRDO development timeline for a complex robotic system?
quizA: Often 8-12 years from requirement to deployment — longer than commercial timelines because of indigenous-component sourcing, military-grade qualification, and field testing.
mindBlow: Daksh was DRDO's first robotics product to enter active military service. Multiple variants have since been exported to friendly nations, and the same engineering team has spun off armed UGV and surveillance robot programmes.
body:
DRDO's robotics work is concentrated at R&DE(E) Pune (Daksh family, EOD), NSTL Visakhapatnam (underwater systems including the Mareech anti-torpedo decoy), and various establishments developing UAVs. The model is research-and-prototyping with eventual transfer to Bharat Electronics, Bharat Forge, or private partners for serial production.

The Daksh story is the most successful DRDO robotics commercialisation to date. The platform went from prototype in the mid-2000s to active service by 2010, then expanded through multiple variants. Indian Army EOD teams use it regularly in counter-IED operations.

Future DRDO robotics priorities include armed UGVs (for border surveillance and convoy escort), micro-aerial vehicles for soldier-level reconnaissance, swarm-drone capability (demonstrated under ALFA-S in 2021), and increasingly AI-assisted target recognition. The deeper trend is more partnership with Indian private industry — startups like ideaForge, Garuda Aerospace, NewSpace Research, and BluJay Solutions increasingly win DRDO and Indian Army contracts.

title: IIT Robotics Research
oneLiner: Cutting-edge robotics research from IIT campuses across India.
layman: IIT robotics labs are India's premier centres for academic robotics research — building research robots, publishing papers in top conferences (ICRA, IROS), and training the next generation of Indian roboticists.
analogy: Think of IIT robotics labs as India's Stanford-equivalents for robotics — small but excellent groups pushing the boundaries of what robots can do.
why: Academic research is where the next generation of robotics talent is trained and where breakthrough ideas originate. IIT labs feed both the Indian robotics industry and the global research community.
indianEx: IIT Bombay's AUV team has built four generations of autonomous underwater vehicles competing internationally. IIT Madras's CFI hosts multiple robotics teams (AUV, Raftaar formula-student-AI, Avishkar hyperloop). IIT Kanpur's robotics group produces ICRA papers in manipulation and locomotion.
quizQ: Which Indian academic institution has produced the most ICRA/IROS papers?
quizA: IIT Bombay leads, followed by IIT Madras, IIT Kanpur, and IISc Bengaluru. All four together produce 30-50 papers per year at top robotics venues.
mindBlow: IIT Madras's CFI (Centre for Innovation) houses student teams that have competed at — and sometimes won — global competitions in autonomous underwater, hyperloop, formula racing, and Mars-rover challenges.
body:
IIT robotics research spans every IIT campus and several other institutions (IISc Bengaluru, IIIT Hyderabad, IIIT Delhi). The strongest concentrations are at IIT Bombay (manipulation, AUVs, drones), IIT Madras (CFI's student teams, control theory), IIT Kanpur (humanoid robotics, computer vision), IIT Delhi (cognitive robotics, ROS-based platforms), and IIT Kharagpur (legged robots, swarm systems).

The quality is improving steadily. Indian groups now place papers regularly at ICRA, IROS, and RSS — historically dominated by US, German, and Japanese labs. Several IIT spinoffs (Niqo Robotics, Niramai, GridBots, Genrobotics, Sastra) are real businesses with international customers.

The student-led robotics teams are arguably as important as faculty research. AUV-IITB, IIT Madras Mars Rover, IIT Kharagpur AGV, and dozens of similar teams train hundreds of students per year in genuine engineering — building things that actually have to work at competitions in San Diego, Utah, or Munich. These alumni populate India's robotics startup landscape and global tech companies' robotics teams.

title: GreyOrange
oneLiner: India's warehouse robotics unicorn — Butler AMRs deployed across 12 countries.
layman: GreyOrange is a Gurugram-based startup that builds the Butler robot — an autonomous mobile robot that carries shelves to human pickers in warehouses. It's India's most successful robotics export.
analogy: GreyOrange is to India what Amazon Robotics (formerly Kiva) is to the US — but smaller, more global in distribution, and faster-growing in non-US markets.
why: GreyOrange is proof that India can build globally competitive product robotics — not just IT services. It's the first Indian robotics company to scale internationally and provides a template for the dozens of Indian robotics startups now following.
indianEx: GreyOrange operates Butler robots at Flipkart's Bengaluru hub, H&M's Dubai distribution centre, Walmart's distribution facilities, and many large 3PLs across Asia, Europe, and the Americas. Founded by Samay Kohli and Akash Gupta in 2011, headquartered in Gurugram with offices in Atlanta and Tokyo.
quizQ: Where was GreyOrange founded and headquartered?
quizA: Founded in Gurugram, India in 2011. Now dual-headquartered in Gurugram and Atlanta, with significant operations also in Tokyo and Singapore.
mindBlow: GreyOrange's Butler robots collectively cover the equivalent of several Indian cricket stadiums per day, moving shelves continuously across global fulfilment centres.
body:
GreyOrange was founded in 2011 by Samay Kohli (IIIT Bangalore graduate) and Akash Gupta as one of India's first product-robotics companies. The flagship Butler robot is a goods-to-person AMR — it slides under a movable shelf-pod, lifts it, and carries it to a stationary picker who selects the required items.

The company's growth was not linear. Multiple pivots, leadership transitions, and capital cycles preceded the current global scale. The Flipkart deployment (formally announced in 2022) stabilised the Indian business and validated the platform domestically. International expansion through partnerships with H&M, Walmart, and major 3PLs followed.

GreyOrange's strategic significance is bigger than its revenue. It's the first Indian robotics company to compete head-to-head with Amazon Robotics, Locus, and Geek+ in global tenders. Its engineering centre in Gurugram is one of India's largest robotics employers, and its alumni have spread to other Indian robotics startups. For Indian robotics, GreyOrange is the existence proof.

title: Addverb Technologies
oneLiner: Noida-based robotics company backed by Reliance Industries.
layman: Addverb builds warehouse robots — autonomous carrying systems, sortation machines, robotic pickers, and pick-to-light systems — manufactured in India and sold to large-scale Indian and international customers.
analogy: If GreyOrange is the export-focused Indian warehouse robotics maker, Addverb is the Indian-market-focused giant — manufacturing in India, with deep enterprise relationships across Reliance's portfolio.
why: Addverb's backing by Reliance Industries means it has effectively guaranteed initial demand from one of Asia's largest conglomerates — and an embedded distribution network. The combination has made it one of the world's fastest-growing warehouse-automation companies by volume.
indianEx: Addverb operates one of India's largest robotics manufacturing facilities at its Noida campus, producing AGVs (Trailer, Veloce), sortation systems (Sortie), pick-to-light arrays, and robotic-arm picking systems. Customers include Reliance Retail, Marico, Flipkart, and global FMCG companies.
quizQ: What's Addverb's biggest backer and customer?
quizA: Reliance Industries — both a strategic investor and a major customer through Reliance Retail's growing fulfilment network.
mindBlow: Addverb's Noida manufacturing facility produces approximately one robot every few hours when running at full capacity — making it among the highest-volume robotics manufacturing plants in India.
body:
Addverb Technologies was founded in 2016 by Sangeet Kumar and three co-founders, with strategic investment from Reliance Industries soon after. The company designs and manufactures the full breadth of warehouse automation hardware — AGVs, AMRs, sortation systems, pick-to-light, automatic-storage-and-retrieval — alongside the software stack that orchestrates them.

The Reliance backing was decisive. It provided initial guaranteed demand (Reliance's retail and pharma businesses), capital for scale-up, and a manufacturing partner. Addverb today operates one of India's most ambitious robotics manufacturing facilities, with capacity to produce thousands of robots per year.

Addverb competes directly with GreyOrange in the Indian and Asian markets, and with Dematic, Honeywell, and Daifuku globally. Its differentiation is full-stack delivery — a customer can buy hardware, software, and integration from one vendor. The growth ahead depends on international expansion (early but underway) and continued vertical integration into adjacent categories.

title: Ati Motors
oneLiner: Bengaluru-based startup building autonomous indoor industrial vehicles.
layman: Ati Motors makes Sherpa — a self-driving electric tug that pulls trolleys around factories and warehouses. It navigates without needing markers on the floor, using cameras and lidar to map its environment.
analogy: Think of a self-driving forklift trained specifically for the inside of Indian factories — narrow aisles, mixed-traffic conditions, dust and heat.
why: Ati is one of India's strongest examples of indoor industrial autonomy — solving real Indian factory and warehouse problems with Indian-built robots that are robust enough for the conditions.
indianEx: Ati Motors' Sherpa vehicles are deployed at multiple Tata, Mahindra, and Bosch manufacturing plants across India, with growing exports to Southeast Asia and the Middle East. Founded by Saurabh Chandra (former auto-industry executive) in Bengaluru in 2017.
quizQ: What makes Sherpa different from traditional AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles)?
quizA: Sherpa doesn't need floor markers, magnetic tape, or fixed paths. It uses [SLAM](/atlas/concept/slam) and onboard sensors to navigate freely — like a self-driving car, but for factories.
mindBlow: Ati's Sherpa can navigate an Indian factory floor — with constantly changing layouts, parked trolleys, and moving workers — at near-pedestrian speed without ever needing the floor to be modified.
body:
Ati Motors was founded in Bengaluru in 2017 by Saurabh Chandra and partners, focused on a specific problem: replacing diesel tow-tractors in factories with electric, autonomous, navigation-capable vehicles. The Sherpa platform was designed to be robust for Indian industrial conditions — heat, dust, narrow corridors, mixed traffic with humans.

The technology stack uses [LIDAR](/atlas/concept/lidar), depth cameras, and [SLAM](/atlas/concept/slam) for navigation. The vehicle creates a map on first deployment, then operates autonomously thereafter — no infrastructure modification needed. This is the key difference from older AGVs that required magnetic tape or floor markers.

Ati's customer base concentrates in automotive (Tata, Mahindra, Maruti suppliers) and manufacturing (Bosch, Foxconn India), with growing presence in pharma and food. The platform exports to Southeast Asia, Middle East, and recently the EU. Ati is one of the cleanest examples of an Indian deep-tech robotics startup that is competing globally on capability — not just price.

title: Sastra Robotics
oneLiner: Kerala startup automating quality testing with robotic arms.
layman: Sastra Robotics builds small robotic arms that automate the testing of electronics — tapping touchscreens, pressing buttons, swiping gestures — to verify that mobile phones, dashboards, and consumer electronics behave correctly.
analogy: Imagine a tireless robot finger that taps your phone 10,000 times in a row to test if the touchscreen works perfectly — that's what Sastra's robots do, but for industrial QA.
why: Mobile phones, automotive infotainment systems, and consumer electronics need millions of test interactions before launch. Manual testing is slow and inconsistent. Sastra's robots replace human testers with deterministic, repeatable testing.
indianEx: Sastra Robotics' arms are used by Microsoft, Bosch, Samsung, and other global manufacturers for HMI (Human-Machine Interface) testing. Founded by Aronin Ponnappan in Kochi, Kerala — one of the few major robotics startups outside India's traditional tech corridors.
quizQ: What category does Sastra Robotics primarily serve?
quizA: HMI test automation — using small precise robotic arms to test touchscreens, knobs, buttons, and gestures in mobile phones, automotive dashboards, and consumer electronics.
mindBlow: Sastra's robots can perform over 1 million touch interactions per device test cycle — work that would take a human tester months to complete with significantly higher error rate.
body:
Sastra Robotics was founded in Kochi, Kerala — a deliberate choice that demonstrates Indian robotics is not concentrated only in Bengaluru-Gurugram. The company specialises in HMI test automation: small, precise robotic arms with calibrated finger-shaped end effectors that interact with electronics under test exactly like a human user would.

The customer category is global. Mobile phone makers (Samsung, Lenovo), automotive Tier-1 suppliers (Bosch, Continental), and consumer-electronics OEMs use Sastra arms to validate touchscreen response, gesture recognition, and button-press behaviour at scale. The economic argument is straightforward — a single test cycle requires hundreds of thousands of interactions, and humans cannot do this consistently.

Sastra's significance for Indian robotics is doubled by its location and its customer profile. A Kerala-based company selling to Microsoft and Samsung normalises the idea that high-end robotics can come from anywhere in India, and that Indian-built precision robots can compete on technical merit, not just price.

title: Niramai
oneLiner: AI + thermal imaging startup revolutionising cancer screening in India.
layman: Niramai uses a thermal camera plus AI software to screen for breast cancer — without radiation, without physical contact, and at much lower cost than traditional mammography. It's particularly relevant for India where mammography access is limited outside metros.
analogy: Imagine a smartphone-like device that can detect breast cancer with 90%+ accuracy by taking thermal pictures — and works for women of all ages, including the under-40s where mammography is less effective.
why: Breast cancer is the leading cancer in Indian women, and Indian mortality is higher than developed countries primarily because of late detection. Niramai's technology dramatically expands screening access — particularly to younger women and tier-2/3 cities.
indianEx: Niramai Health Analytics is based in Bengaluru. Founded in 2016 by Dr. Geetha Manjunath. Deployed in over 90 hospitals across India and 7 countries. Won the WHO Technology Innovations Award.
quizQ: Why is Niramai's approach particularly useful for younger Indian women?
quizA: Mammography works less well on dense breast tissue typical in younger women. Niramai's thermal-AI approach is effective across age groups, including the under-40s where mammography sensitivity drops.
mindBlow: Niramai detects breast tumours as small as 5mm — earlier than the smallest detectable size on most clinical mammography systems used in Indian hospitals.
body:
Niramai (the name comes from a Sanskrit phrase meaning 'free from illness') was founded in Bengaluru in 2016 by Dr. Geetha Manjunath, a former Xerox PARC and HP Labs researcher. The product, SMILE-100, combines a high-resolution thermal camera with proprietary AI software trained on tens of thousands of patient cases.

The medical proposition is that breast tumours generate heat signatures detectable in thermal images well before they become palpable or visible on standard mammography. Niramai's algorithm distinguishes cancerous from benign signatures with reported sensitivity of 70-90% depending on study and patient population.

The Indian impact is potentially enormous. Mammography requires expensive equipment, trained radiologists, and travel to urban hospitals — none of which scale to India's tier-2 and tier-3 cities. Niramai's system is portable, lower-cost, requires no radiation, and produces a digital screen that an AI assists with. Deployment partnerships with NGOs, government screening programmes, and corporate health benefits have expanded coverage substantially. Niramai is one of India's clearest examples of robotics-adjacent AI built for Indian conditions and exported successfully.

title: Agri-Robotics India
oneLiner: India's robot farming revolution — drones, weeders, harvesters.
layman: Indian agri-robotics covers crop-spraying drones, precision weeding robots, soil-mapping drones, milking-shed automation, and emerging fruit-picking and grading robots. Each addresses a specific farming bottleneck.
analogy: Imagine a farmer's toolkit that includes drones for spraying, cameras to scout for disease, robotic weeders, and software that tells you exactly what each plant needs — that's the assembled stack of Indian agri-robotics today.
why: Indian agriculture employs 60% of the workforce but produces low value-add per hectare. Robotics is one of the few practical levers to raise productivity, reduce chemical use, and create higher-value rural jobs.
indianEx: Garuda Aerospace (Chennai), Marut Drones (Hyderabad), Niqo Robotics (Bengaluru), and several smaller startups operate in this space. Garuda has DGCA approval for crop-spraying drones used by over 100,000 Indian farmers; Niqo's spot-sprayers are reducing chemical use by 80% in cotton.
quizQ: What's the single largest near-term opportunity in Indian agri-robotics?
quizA: Drone-based crop spraying — supported by DGCA's Drone Rules 2021, subsidised under the Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization, and economically validated in cotton, paddy, and sugarcane.
mindBlow: A single agri-drone can spray 1 hectare in 7 minutes — work that takes a human with a backpack sprayer 7 hours, with much higher exposure to pesticides.
body:
Indian agri-robotics has matured rapidly since 2021. The triggers were favourable drone regulations (DGCA Drone Rules 2021), the PLI scheme for drones, and the Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization which subsidises drone purchases for FPOs (Farmer Producer Organisations) up to 40%.

The category breaks down into spraying drones (largest by volume — Garuda, Marut, ioCrops), scouting and mapping drones (used by seed companies, FPOs, and insurance assessors), spot-spraying ground robots (Niqo, John Deere's See & Spray for export), milking automation, and emerging fruit-grading robots. The most-deployed product is the crop-spraying drone, with over 100,000 Indian farmers reportedly served as of 2024.

The deeper opportunity is integration. A scouting drone identifies weed clusters → a spot-spray robot or drone treats them → output data flows into a farm-management system → next season's plan is informed by this season's analytics. The Indian agri-robotics stack is being built, and several Indian startups will likely emerge as global category leaders within the decade.

title: Make in India Robotics
oneLiner: Government push to manufacture robots and robot components in India.
layman: Make in India is a government initiative to make India a manufacturing hub. For robotics, it includes specific schemes (PLI for drones, semiconductors, electronics) that subsidise Indian-made robots and robot components.
analogy: Think of Make in India as a set of financial nudges — subsidies and tax breaks — that make it more profitable to build robots in India rather than import them from China, Korea, or Germany.
why: Today most Indian robotics deployments use imported core components (motors, sensors, controllers). This creates strategic vulnerability and pricing pressure. Make in India aims to localise the robotics supply chain.
indianEx: The PLI (Production Linked Incentive) scheme covers drone components (announced 2021), specialty electronics, and increasingly robotics-adjacent categories. Companies like Garuda, ideaForge, and Bharat Forge have used PLI benefits to expand Indian manufacturing.
quizQ: Which PLI scheme is most directly relevant to robotics?
quizA: The drone and drone-components PLI (₹120 crore over 3 years, launched 2021) is the most direct. Adjacent schemes for electronics components and semiconductor manufacturing also benefit the robotics supply chain.
mindBlow: Make in India's PLI schemes have collectively committed over ₹1.97 lakh crore (~$24 billion) to Indian manufacturing across 14 sectors — a fraction of which directly or indirectly supports robotics localisation.
body:
Make in India was launched in 2014 as a broad manufacturing-promotion initiative. Its robotics-relevant elements include the PLI schemes (Production Linked Incentives covering drones, electronics, semiconductors), the National Single Window System for clearances, and structural reforms in defence procurement that favour Indian-made systems.

For robotics specifically, the highest-impact policies have been: (1) DGCA's Drone Rules 2021 and the parallel drone PLI scheme, which created a real Indian drone industry virtually overnight; (2) the semiconductor PLI announced in 2021, supporting domestic chip manufacturing that downstream benefits sensor and microcontroller production; (3) defence-procurement reforms that prioritise indigenous robotics systems for the Indian military.

The gaps are still significant. Most precision motors, servo controllers, and high-end sensors are still imported. Robotic-grade lithium batteries, brushless motors with encoder feedback, and industrial-grade controllers are areas where Indian manufacturing is at an early stage. Future PLI extensions and import substitution programmes are likely to target these gaps over the next five years.

title: Atal Innovation Mission
oneLiner: NITI Aayog's flagship programme funding 10,000 school tinkering labs and innovation hubs.
layman: Atal Innovation Mission (AIM) is a government programme that has built over 10,000 Atal Tinkering Labs in Indian schools, plus innovation hubs and incubators. Each ATL gets ₹20 lakh of robotics, electronics, and 3D printing equipment.
analogy: AIM is like a national scholarship for hands-on learning — instead of giving students individual grants, the government gave entire schools the equipment they need to build robots.
why: AIM is the largest single distribution of robotics-learning equipment anywhere in the world. If even a fraction of these labs become productive, India's robotics talent pipeline transforms.
indianEx: Over 10,000 Atal Tinkering Labs operate across Indian schools as of 2024. AIM also runs Atal Incubation Centres in major universities, the ATL Marathon competition, and the Atal Tinkerpreneur programme.
quizQ: How much equipment does each ATL receive?
quizA: Approximately ₹20 lakh worth of equipment — including 3D printers, Arduino kits, robotics modules, electronics prototyping gear, and tools. Renewals and upgrades follow annual government allocations.
mindBlow: If you summed up the equipment in all 10,000+ ATLs, the total exceeds ₹2,000 crore — making AIM the world's largest school-level robotics infrastructure deployment.
body:
The Atal Innovation Mission was launched in 2016 under NITI Aayog (the government's policy think-tank). Its core programmes are: (1) Atal Tinkering Labs in schools — over 10,000 built; (2) Atal Incubation Centres at universities and tech parks — 70+ operating; (3) ANIC (Atal New India Challenges) for innovation grants; (4) ATL Marathon, the annual nationwide robotics competition.

The ATL programme is the largest by scale and the most directly relevant to robotics. Each lab receives equipment and a structured curriculum. Schools must demonstrate active utilisation to qualify for renewals. The actual outcomes vary widely — some ATL schools win national competitions and produce students who go on to IIT robotics clubs; many others run barely-used labs.

AIM's impact is greater than the equipment value because of the cultural shift it created. A generation of Indian students grew up understanding that building a robot is something they could actually do, not something only IIT graduates have access to. The next decade will reveal whether AIM produces a comparable wave of Indian robotics founders — early signs are encouraging.

title: e-Yantra
oneLiner: IIT Bombay's free national robotics competition for college students.
layman: e-Yantra is a free, government-funded robotics competition run by IIT Bombay. College students from across India form teams, get free or subsidised robotics kits, and compete over four months on themed robotics projects. IIT Bombay faculty mentor every team.
analogy: Think of e-Yantra as India's national-scale robotics bootcamp — IIT Bombay teaches the methods, supplies the kits, hosts the competition, and provides the certification.
why: e-Yantra is the single most accessible high-quality robotics training programme for Indian college students. Alumni populate IIT robotics clubs, ISRO, DRDO, and most Indian robotics startups. It bypasses the institutional inequality of Indian engineering colleges.
indianEx: Run by IIT Bombay's Department of Computer Science and Engineering, funded by the Ministry of Education's NMEICT initiative. Tens of thousands of college students have participated since 2010.
quizQ: What does e-Yantra cost a participant?
quizA: Mostly free or heavily subsidised. The kit (typically ₹4,000-8,000) is often free for selected teams; mentoring from IIT Bombay is included; the competition itself is free.
mindBlow: e-Yantra has trained over 100,000 college students in robotics through its competitions and online courses — making it one of the largest robotics-education programmes anywhere in the world.
body:
e-Yantra is IIT Bombay's flagship robotics-education programme, funded by the Ministry of Education through NMEICT (National Mission on Education through Information and Communication Technology). It launched in 2010 and has since trained over 100,000 college students nationwide.

The annual competition cycle starts in August with a themed problem (often inspired by real industrial or research challenges — agricultural robot navigation, supply chain optimisation, hospital service robots). Selected teams receive a kit, training videos, and access to IIT Bombay mentors via online forums. Teams develop their solutions over four months, submit video demonstrations and code, and finalists are invited to IIT Bombay for in-person evaluation.

The spillover effects are larger than the competition itself. e-Yantra alumni are heavily represented in IIT Bombay's research robotics teams, in Indian robotics startups (GreyOrange, Niramai, Ati Motors all hire e-Yantra alumni), and increasingly in international PhD programmes. For a college student outside the IIT/NIT system, e-Yantra is the most credible robotics signal on a résumé.

title: NASSCOM Robotics
oneLiner: Industry body tracking India's AI and robotics workforce and policy.
layman: NASSCOM is India's IT/tech industry association. Its AI and robotics arms publish data on hiring trends, run industry events, advocate for policy, and connect Indian robotics companies with each other and with global partners.
analogy: Think of NASSCOM as the industry's lobby group, conference organiser, and research arm rolled together — when Indian robotics companies need to speak with one voice to government, NASSCOM is the channel.
why: NASSCOM's industry data is the most-cited source for Indian robotics and AI workforce projections. Its policy positions influence how India regulates AI, autonomy, and emerging tech.
indianEx: NASSCOM's annual AI Compendium and Centre of Excellence reports document Indian robotics maturity. Major Indian robotics startups (GreyOrange, Addverb, Niramai, ideaForge) are NASSCOM members.
quizQ: What's NASSCOM's most-cited workforce projection for AI/robotics?
quizA: Approximately 1 million Indian AI and robotics professionals needed by 2027 — the figure quoted across most Indian career and policy publications.
mindBlow: NASSCOM was founded in 1988 with 38 members. Today it represents over 3,000 companies, including most major Indian robotics startups.
body:
NASSCOM (National Association of Software and Service Companies) is India's pre-eminent technology industry association. Founded in 1988 to represent Indian IT services, it has expanded over the decades to cover product companies, AI/ML firms, robotics startups, and increasingly hardware-driven deep-tech.

For robotics, NASSCOM provides three main services: (1) workforce and market data — its annual reports document Indian AI and robotics maturity; (2) policy advocacy — engaging with MEITY, NITI Aayog, and ministries on AI, drone, and autonomy regulation; (3) ecosystem programmes — Centres of Excellence focused on IoT, AI/ML, and robotics, which incubate startups and run training programmes.

The industry voice matters when policy is being written. India's AI policy debates, drone regulations, and skilling programmes all involve NASSCOM input. For Indian robotics startups, membership provides access to data, networking, and policy advocacy. For international companies entering India, NASSCOM is the most credible single counterparty for industry-level conversations.

title: Startup India Robotics
oneLiner: Government grants, incubators, and tax benefits supporting Indian robotics startups.
layman: Startup India is a government initiative offering tax breaks, grants, and other benefits to recognised startups. For robotics startups, it provides access to incubators, funding programmes, simplified regulatory compliance, and intellectual property fast-tracking.
analogy: Think of Startup India as the support package — paperwork relief, modest grants, and connection to investors — that the government offers to anyone trying to build a tech startup in India.
why: Robotics startups are capital-intensive and slow to revenue. Startup India's benefits — even when modest — provide critical early-stage financial relief and signal credibility to private investors.
indianEx: Most major Indian robotics startups have used some Startup India benefits: tax holidays under section 80-IAC, recognised-startup status for procurement, Atal Innovation Mission-affiliated incubation, and SIDBI Fund of Funds support.
quizQ: What's the most concrete benefit of Startup India recognition for a robotics startup?
quizA: A 3-year income tax holiday under section 80-IAC for eligible startups, simplified regulatory compliance, and access to incubators and government procurement preferences.
mindBlow: Over 100,000 Indian startups are recognised under Startup India as of 2024 — collectively raising over $100 billion in funding since 2016.
body:
Startup India launched in 2016 as a broad initiative to support new businesses. Its provisions include tax holidays (3 years of income-tax exemption under section 80-IAC), self-certification for labour and environmental laws, a fast-track patent examination scheme, and the Fund of Funds for Startups operated by SIDBI.

For robotics startups specifically, the most useful programmes are: (1) Atal Innovation Mission-affiliated incubators (free or subsidised office space, mentoring, sometimes seed grants); (2) BIRAC (Biotechnology Industry Research Assistance Council) grants for medical and biotechnology robotics; (3) Defence Innovation Organisation (DIO) and iDEX (Innovations for Defence Excellence) for defence-relevant robotics startups; (4) state-level incubators like Karnataka's K-tech, Telangana's T-Hub, Kerala Startup Mission.

Indian robotics startups that have used these benefits include Niqo Robotics (BIRAC grants), Ati Motors (state incubation in Bengaluru), GreyOrange (Startup India recognition and tax benefits in early years), Genrobotics (Kerala Startup Mission), and many others. The benefits are not transformative individually but together can extend a robotics startup's runway by 12-24 months — often the difference between proving the product and running out of money.
